extra.reasoning:
The property "globally(opted_out implies not sent)" is VIOLATED.

The only transitions that set sent:=true are t2 and t5. t2's guard (not opted_out) ensures opted_out is false when it fires, so it is safe. But t5's guard is (opted_out and ticks >= 10) and it sets sent:=true — this directly establishes a state where opted_out=true AND sent=true, contradicting the property.

t5 is reachable: ticks can be driven to 10 via the tick event (t4 increments ticks up to a cap of 10), and opted_out becomes true via optout (t1).

Counterexample execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
1. optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0
2-11. tick ×10 (t4): increments ticks 0->1->...->10, each also sets sent=false. Result: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
12. send_attempt: with opted_out=true and ticks>=10, transition t5 is enabled and sets sent:=true. Result: opted_out=true, sent=true.

This final state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating "opted_out implies not sent".

Note on rule conflict: on this final send_attempt, both t3 (guard opted_out, sets sent:=false) and t5 (guard opted_out and ticks>=10, sets sent:=true) are enabled. No priority ordering is specified between them, so t5 is a legitimately takeable transition. Since the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, the existence of an execution that takes t5 and reaches the bad state suffices to violate the property. t5 is essentially a buggy "override" that re-enables sending after opt-out once the tick counter saturates.
